coredata.dc:title: Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots induced by acidic potassium permanganate and its application to quenchometric flow-injection assays of hydroquinone in water
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Ming
Chen, Peiyun
Dong, Yajuan
Sun, Hanwen
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with particle size of 4.5±1.0nm were prepared and characterized by transmission electron microscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. It was found that KMnO4 could oxidize GQDs to produce a relatively intense chemiluminescence (CL) emission. The mechanism of CL generation was investigated based on absorption spectra and CL emission spectra. CL emission was attributed to the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and thermally excited electrons in the GQDs. On the other hand, both KMnO4 and ∙O2 − could react with GQDs to produce GQDs∙+ and GQDs∙−. The electron-transfer annihilation of GQDs∙+ and GQDs∙− could form excited-state GQDs*, which acted as the final emitter in the system. In order to show the analytic potential of GQDs–KMnO4 CL system, it was applied for the determination of hydroquinone based on its diminishing effect. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed CL system exhibits excellent analytic performance for determination of hydroquinone. Calibration curve in the range of 2.49×10−4–9.96×10−7 gmL−1 was linear with the correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9924. The limit of detection was 8.46×10−8 gmL−1, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 1.7% for 11 determinations of 4.98×10−6 gmL−1 hydroquinone. The applicability of the method was verified by applying to real tap water, lake water, and waste water samples. The recoveries were in the range of 89.7–97.1% with RSD of 0.9–2.1%. The proposed method has a good linearity, high sensitivity and good repeatability and can be applied for routine determination of hydroquinone in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Chemiluminescence
Potassium permanganate
Hydroquinone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Choice of oxidant and optimization of CL reaction conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of flow injection chemiluminescence analysis system, P—peristaltic pump; V—sampling inlet valve; F—flowing cell: a flat spiral-coiled colorless glass tube (i.d. 1.0mm, total diameter of the flow cell 3cm, without gas between loops); PMT—photomultiplier tube; AMP—amplifier; PC—recorder; W—waste; a—mixture solution of GQDs; b—H2SO4 solution; c—KMnO4 solution; d—sample solution.
TEM image of the prepared GQDs, and the structure of GQDs.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs and (B) fluorescence spectra for GQDs excited at wavelengths from 300 to 425nm in 25nm increments. The insets are their photographs of GQDs under 365nm UV light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Effect of concentration of KMnO4 and H2SO4 on chemiluminescence intensity.
(A) CL spectrum of GQDs+KMnO4 reaction systems in acid conditions and (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of (a) KMnO4, (b) hydroquinone, (c) KMnO4+hydroquinone, and (d) GQDs+KMnO4.
A schematic of the CL reaction mechanism for GQDs–KMnO4 system.
Kinetic curve of the CL reaction system a—125μL GQDs+100μL H2SO4+2mL KMnO4, b—125μL GQDs+100μL H2SO4+2mL KMnO4, c—125μL GQDs+100μL H2SO4+2mL KMnO4+1mL hydroquinone.
Recovery of hydroquinone from spiked blank water samples.

head.ce:title: Chemiluminescence of graphene quantum dots induced by acidic potassium permanganate and its application to quenchometric flow-injection assays of hydroquinone in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Hebei University, Key Laboratory of Analytical Science and Technology of Hebei Province, Baoding 071002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Science, Hebei University, Key Laboratory of Analytical Science and Technology of Hebei Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with particle size of 4.5±1.0nm were prepared and characterized by transmission electron microscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. It was found that KMnO4 could oxidize GQDs to produce a relatively intense chemiluminescence (CL) emission. The mechanism of CL generation was investigated based on absorption spectra and CL emission spectra. CL emission was attributed to the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and thermally excited electrons in the GQDs. On the other hand, both KMnO4 and ∙O2 − could react with GQDs to produce GQDs∙+ and GQDs∙−. The electron-transfer annihilation of GQDs∙+ and GQDs∙− could form excited-state GQDs*, which acted as the final emitter in the system. In order to show the analytic potential of GQDs–KMnO4 CL system, it was applied for the determination of hydroquinone based on its diminishing effect. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed CL system exhibits excellent analytic performance for determination of hydroquinone. Calibration curve in the range of 2.49×10−4–9.96×10−7 gmL−1 was linear with the correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9924. The limit of detection was 8.46×10−8 gmL−1, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 1.7% for 11 determinations of 4.98×10−6 gmL−1 hydroquinone. The applicability of the method was verified by applying to real tap water, lake water, and waste water samples. The recoveries were in the range of 89.7–97.1% with RSD of 0.9–2.1%. The proposed method has a good linearity, high sensitivity and good repeatability and can be applied for routine determination of hydroquinone in water.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemiluminescence (CL) can be produced by direct oxidation of target compound to produce emitting species or by indirect enhancing or inhibitory effects of certain luminescence compounds. Metal-based quantum dots were used for enhanced CL analysis [1–3]. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are new fluorescent carbon nanomaterial. The CQDs are always spherical with obvious crystal lattice. The GQDs possess single or few layers graphene and connected chemical groups on the edge. They are anisotropic with lateral dimension larger than the height. GQDs have gained tremendous attention owing to their unique and tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, exceptional physicochemical properties, high photostability, biocompatibility, and small size [4]. These properties make them much more desirable for many applications compared with inorganic semiconductor QDs [5,6]. However, there were only two reports on application of GQDs in CL analysis. Ce(IV) can directly oxidize GQDs to give rise to CL emission at 470nm and the CL was applied for the determination of uric acid [7]. Hypochlorite can directly oxidize GQDs to give rise to CL emission at 510nm which was applied for the determination of chlorine [8]. To the best of our knowledge, the research on the CL properties of GQDs-oxidant is still in an early stage and the full potential of this material has not yet been fathomed.

Hydroquinone is widely used in tanning, cosmetics, the pharmaceutical industry, and in developing photographs, it is important environmental pollutants. It is harmful to humans and animals even in very low concentration. Therefore, it is very important to develop a highly sensitive method for the determination of hydroquinone in environmental waters. Wang et al. reviewed in 2015 the research progress of the major methods for detecting hydroquinone, including chromatography, spectrometry, chemiluminescence and electrochemistry [9]. Recently, Elghobashy et al. described successive ratio subtraction coupled with constant multiplication spectrophotometric method for determination of hydroquinone in complex mixture with its degradation products [10]. Although some fluorometric and colorimetric chemosensors have been developed for detection of cyanide ions and anionic species [11,12], there was only two papers for the determination of hydroquinone by the luminol–K3Fe(CN)6–ZnSe quantum dots CL system [13] and luminol–Co(II)–H2O2–CdTe quantum dots CL system [14]. To our knowledge there was no report for the application of GQDs-CL in hydroquinone analysis.

This work is to investigate the UV absorption, fluorescence and direct CL properties of the GQDs induced by KMnO4. A mechanism of GQDs–KMnO4 CL reaction was suggested based on the CL emission spectra and the effects of radical ∙O2 − on the CL intensity. Finally, the application potential of this CL system was investigated. A sensitive flow-injection GQDs–KMnO4 CL method without any luminescence reagent for the selective determination of hydroquinone in water samples was developed.

WP800T Microwave oven working at 2450MHz with an output power of 800W (Galanz Electrical Appliances Enterprise Co., Ltd., Shongde, China) and 84−1 magnetic stirring temperature control electric heating sleeve (Shandong Juancheng Hualu Electric Instrument Co., Ltd.) was used to synthesize GQDs. A Double Light Spectrophotometer (Beijing TAYASAF Science & Technology Co., Ltd, China), GL-9406 portable UV reflectance meter (Haimen kylin medical instrument factory, China) and F-7000 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) were used to investigate the characteristics of GQDs and mechanism of CL emission. An IFFM-E flow-injection CL analysis system (Remex Electronic Sci-Tech. Co. Ltd, Xi׳an, China) was used for CL analysis.

Poly(ethylene glycol) with average molecular weight of 200 (PEG 200) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Citric acid (99.5%), sodium hydroxide (96.0%), sulfuric acid(98.0%), potassium permanganate (99.5%), glutathione (98%), and hydroquinone (99.0%), were purchased form Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Diperiodato-nicklate(IV), diperiodato-cuprate(III), and diperiodato-argenate(III) were prepared by oxidizing Ni(II), Cu(II), and Ag(I) in alkaline solutions according to the literature [15]. Hydroquinone stock solutions, 2.59×10−3 gmL−1 was prepared and stored in a refrigerator at 4°C to keep dark. More dilute solutions were prepared freshly by diluting the stock solution with deionized water.

GQDs were synthesized according to the literature [16]. Briefly, 2g citric acid was put into a 5mL beaker and heated to 200°C using a heating mantle. About 5min later, the citric acid was melted. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange in 30min, implying the formation of GQDs. Concentration and dialysis were used to purify the obtained products. 100mL of 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution was added into the orange liquid drop wise with continuous and vigorous stirring. The obtained solutions (adjusted to pH 7) were stable for at least one month in the refrigerator at 4°C.

A flow-injection CL analysis system is shown in Fig. 1. Sampling volume 120μL and flow rate 3.0mL/min. Calibration curve for the quenching value of CL intensity (peak height) versus logarithm of analyte concentration was constructed for the analysis of real samples.

The size and the edge-shape effects of GQDs are significant for the visible light absorption properties of GQDs. The sizes of GQDs were controlled via varying the reaction temperatures. The edge-shape effect is enhanced with an increase in size of square GQDs [17]. In this work, a simple carbonization method was used for synthesis of GQDs. The TEM image of the GQDs is shown in Fig. 2. GQDs present nearly monodisperse nanoparticles with particle size of 4.5±1.0nm for 100 GQDs.

The photographs inserted in Fig. 3 shows the prepared GQDs exhibit a bright blue emission under ultraviolet radiation (365nm). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the prepared GQDs exhibits typical absorption of graphene derivatives in the UV region (200–300nm) which was assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [18]. Another broad band at ~345nm was also observed, which is associated with the n–π* transition of C=O bond of surface carboxylic groups [19].

In the present work, the optical properties of the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the GQDS were explored (Fig. 3). When excited at 300–425nm with increments of 25nm, the GQDS exhibit an excitation-dependent PL emission, this implies the uniformity of both the size and the surface state of those sp2 clusters contained in GQDs, which is responsible for the fluorescence of GQDS. A corresponding PL emission peak is broad due to the wide size distribution of GQDS. Moreover, the emission wavelength red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength. The PL emission peak should be attributed to the n→π* transition of C=O bond of surface carboxylic groups [20]. The maximum PL emission at ~470nm for the prepared GQDs was found with an excitation wavelength of 400nm [7]. The spectroscopic properties of GQDs may vary depending on the method of preparation and the functional groups at the edges of the particles. As compared with the metal-based quantum dots, GQDs showed low-cost, low toxicity, highly soluble in various solvents and can be equipped with functional groups at their edges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice of oxidant and optimization of CL reaction conditions
ce:section.ce:para: A relatively strong CL emission of GQDs was induced by direct chemical oxidation with Ce(IV) [7] and hypochlorite [8]. In this work, the CL reactions between GQDs and some oxidants, including K3Fe(CN)6, H2O2, Ag(III), Cu(III), Ni(IV), Ce(IV), and KMnO4, in sulfuric acid medium were investigated. All of these oxidants are able to directly elicit a CL from GQDs. The CL produced using KMnO4 was much more intense than that of the other oxidants. The chemical energy generated during the redox reaction of GQDs with KMnO4 is more matched with the excitation energy required for the formation of the excited-state GQDs* than the other oxidants. So KMnO4 was selected as the best oxidant for the CL reaction.

The effect of KMnO4 concentration on the CL intensity over the range of 1×10−6–8×10−6 M was examined further. Fig. 4A shows that the CL intensity of GQDs increased remarkably with the increase of KMnO4 concentration from 3×10−3 M to 9×10−3 M, and decreased after 9×10−3 M, so this amount was selected as the optimum concentration. The CL intensities were also dependent on the concentration of sulfuric acid. The influence of sulfuric acid concentration in the range of 0.75–2.0M was studied. Fig. 4B shows that the CL response was increased remarkably by increasing sulfuric acid concentration up to 1.5M, over which the relative CL intensity did not increase obviously. So, 1.5M of sulfuric acid was chosen as the optimum value for consequent experiments.

The CL emission was attributed to the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and thermally excited electrons in the GQDs. Oxidants, KMnO4, could inject holes into the GQDs. The injection of holes into the GQDs by oxidants increases the holes in the GQDs, and accelerates the electrons–hole annihilation, which results in energy release in the form of CL emission.

In order to identify the emitting species in the CL reaction, the CL emission spectra of GQDs reaction systems were observed by an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (taken off lamp house). The obtained CL spectra in acid condition are shown in Fig. 5A.

Obviously, maximum CL wavelength of GQDs was at ~525nm. A paper reported direct CL of GQDs induced by hypochlorite at 510nm [8]. The difference of the maximum CL wavelength is possible due to the size and edge-shape effects in the prepared GQDs. Consequently, the CL mechanism for the CL reaction between GQDs and KMnO4 was suggested to involve the formation of excited-state GQDs (GQDs*) through electron-transfer annihilation [7,21]. The GQDs* acted as luminophors to yield CL.

On the other hand, superoxide anion radical formed by dissolved oxygen in the presence of GQDs as a reducing agent may act as an electron injector to GQDs, producing GQDs∙−. GQDs can be oxidased by MnO4 − in acid medium to GQDs∙+. The electron-transfer annihilation of GQDs∙+ and GQDs∙− could form excited-state GQDs*, which were relaxed by electron–hole recombination to produce CL [20,22]. To further confirm this pathway, the effect of deoxygenation on the CL intensity of GQDs–KMnO4 system was investigated. It was found that upon deoxygenation of solution by N2 bubbling for 5min, the CL intensity decreased by about 5.8%, which indicated that the dissolved oxygen had little effect on the CL.

Based on these facts, a schematic of the CL mechanism is shown as Fig. 6, and the CL reaction can be described in its simplest form as follows:
KMnO4+H++GQDs+O2→Mn(II)+GQDs*+ O2∙−
GQDs*→GQDs+hv (525nm)
GQDs+O2∙−→GQDs∙−
MnO4 −+H++GQDs→Mn(II)+GQDs∙+
GQDs∙++GQDs∙−→GQDs*
GQDs*→GQDs+hv (525nm)

After addition of KMnO4 to hydroquinone solution, the UV absorption peak of hydroquinone blue-shifted and the peak height increased (Fig. 5B), showing the reacting of hydroquinone with KMnO4. The target analyte has an inhibitory effect on the GQDs–KMnO4 CL system due to competitive reaction of the analyte with the GQDs.

The CL kinetic characteristics of the reactions system were investigated in detail. The investigation of the CL intensity–time profiles was performed with the static CL analysis. The result is shown in Fig. 6. It was shown that the reaction rate in solution was very fast, from reagent mixing to peak maximum only 2s was needed. The kinetic curve also indicated that the CL of GQDs–KMnO4 system could be inhibited obviously by hydroquinone. The CL properties can be employed as a determination approach for hydroquinone. ( Fig. 7).

In order to explore the analytical applicability of the GQDs–KMnO4 CL system, the effects of some common inorganic ions and organic compounds on the determination of 1.78×10−6 gmL−1 hydroquinone were investigated. The tolerable concentration ratios for interference in relative error of ±5% were over 1000-fold for
NO
3
−
,
Cl
–
,
SO
4
2 –
,
PO
4
3 –
, 200-fold for glucose, starch, citric acid, lactose, dextrin, PEG 200, Mg2+, EDTA, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Br−, and 75-fold for ascorbic acid, Fe3+, and Co2+. The amounts of most potentially interfering species in tap water, lake water, and waste water are below their tolerable levels, so there would be no interference from these species in hydroquinone determination. These results demonstrate that the method possesses a good selectivity for the determination hydroquinone in water.

Under the optimized conditions, calibration curve for the quenching value of CL intensity toward logarithm of analyte concentration conforms to the equation ΔI=4155.81+95.14·logC, which was linear in the range of 2.49×10−4–9.96×10−7 gmL−1, with the correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9924. The limit of detection (LOD) was determined as the sample concentration that produces a peak with a height three times of the level of baseline noise. The LOD was 8.46×10−8 gmL−1, The high assay sensitivity allowed a high fold dilution of the samples before analysis to avoid sample matrix effects. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 1.7% for 11 determinations of 4.98×10−6 gmL−1 hydroquinone.

The sensitivity of this method is better than that of spectrophotometric method [10], electrochemistric method with graphene doped carbon ionic liquid electrode [23], with boron-doped graphene as electrocatalyst [24], and with reduced graphene oxide and multiwall carbon nanotubes hybrid [25], as well as CL method using Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles [26]. Otherwise, it is equivalent to that of CL method [27] and differential pulse voltammetric method [28]. Although the sensitivity is lower than that of the luminol–K3Fe(CN)6–ZnSe quantum dots CL system [13] and luminol–Co(II)–H2O2–CdTe quantum dots CL system [14], the proposed method has low cost, without use of any luminescence reagent.

EDTA of 0.1g was added in 10mL of tap water, lake water and waste water in order to shield interference ions. Under the optimized conditions the proposed method was applied for the determination of hydroquinone in tap water, lake water, and waste water. No hydroquinone was detected by this method and the reported CL method in reference [29]. To examine the method accuracy, recovery test of the assays for the analyte was determined by adding known amounts of the analyte to the samples. Three spiked levels (0.89, 5.34, and 10.7×10−6 gmL−1) were selected for recovery test in triplicate. The result is listed in Table 1.
The obtained recoveries were 91.0–101% with RSD of 1.8–2.1% for tap water, 89.7–93.3% with RSD of 1.2–1.8% for lake water, and 89.6–97.1% with RSD of 0.9–1.8% for waste water.

It is demonstrated that GQDs could be directly oxidized by KMnO4 to give rise to a relatively strong CL emission, without use of any luminescence reagent. As compared with the metal-based quantum dots and inorganic semiconductor QDs, the graphene quantum dots (GQDs) shows low toxicity, highly soluble in various solvents, cost-effective synthesis, simplicity, and practicality. As compared with the luminol–K3Fe(CN)6–ZnSe quantum dots CL system and luminol–Co(II)–H2O2–CdTe quantum dots CL system for the determination of hydroquinone [13,14], the proposed CL system of GQDs induced by acidic potassium permanganate has low toxicity, low cost, without use of any luminescence reagent. The sensitivity of this method is better than that of spectrophotometric method [10], electrochemistric method [23–25], as well as CL method [26]. The emission of the proposed CL system was attributed to the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and thermally excited electrons in the GQDs. The thermally excited electrons annihilate with the KMnO4-injected holes to produce the excited-state GQDs*, which acts as the final emitter in the CL system. On the other hand, both KMnO4 and ∙O2 − could react with GQDs to produce GQDs∙+ and GQDs∙−. The electron-transfer annihilation of GQDs∙+ and GQDs∙− could form excited-state GQDs*, which acted as the final emitter in the system. A new analytic method developed based on its diminishing effect on the GQDs–KMnO4 CL system has a good linearity, high sensitivity and good repeatability and can be applied for routine determination of hydroquinone in tap water, lake water, and waste water samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science Foundation Office of Hebei Province (B2014201171).
